feed.title: Prohibit Merkle Internal Node Preimages That Encode Minimal 64-Byte Transactions

entry.title: Prohibit Merkle Internal Node Preimages That Encode Minimal 64-Byte Transactions
entry.summary:
The discussion revolves around the concept of manipulating blockchain proof systems by creating false transaction outputs that appear legitimate. This technique involves grinding an interior node such that it mimics a transaction with specific characteristics on its left and right parts, such as length fields and scripts tailored to meet particular conditions. Theoretically, this can deceive a proof system into recognizing a non-existent Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO), believing it to be spendable within a specified block.
For instance, if a script includes a condition like "Check Lock Time Verify" (CLTV) for a future block height, the system would be misled into treating the UTXO as unspendable until that block is reached. This approach could enable a sort of "proof of mutex of miner claim," where miners are presented with various transaction offers linked to a UTXO locked at a specific height. The design ensures that a miner can only utilize the UTXO in one transaction, thus having to choose between competing offers, with the others expiring.
Moreover, additional complexities arise if certain opcodes were available, like GETINPUT, which would further enhance the ability to bind transactions conditionally based on input parameters. Such tactics, while theoretically feasible, underscore potential vulnerabilities within blockchain protocols where false connectors could be misused, leading to protocol integrity issues. The proposed rule aims to combat these vulnerabilities by prohibiting any deserializable transaction from being used as an interior node, thus mitigating risks associated with these deceptive practices. This measure seeks to uphold the reliability and security of blockchain transactions, ensuring that only genuine and verifiable transactions are recognized and processed.
